tEn: Why Building a Durable Social Consumer Product Is So Hard
tZh: 为什么打造持久的社交消费产品如此困难
sEn: Evan Spiegel discusses the challenges of building a lasting social consumer product, emphasizing the importance of distribution over product-market fit.
sZh: Evan Spiegel 讨论了打造持久社交消费产品的挑战，强调分发比产品市场契合更重要。

consensus.en: Distribution is the hardest part of building a consumer social product, not product-market fit.
consensus.zh: 分发是构建消费者社交产品最困难的部分，而不是产品市场契合度。

consensus.en: Software is not a moat; ecosystems, platforms, and hardware are harder to copy.
consensus.zh: 软件不是护城河；生态系统、平台和硬件更难复制。

consensus.en: Innovation requires a flat, high-velocity design team that produces many ideas.
consensus.zh: 创新需要一个扁平、高速的设计团队，产出大量想法。

consensus.en: Listening to customers is vital, but don't build exactly what they ask for; empathize and create something new.
consensus.zh: 倾听客户至关重要，但不要完全按他们要求的做；要共情并创造新东西。

consensus.en: Leaders must stay close to customers and products, especially in consumer tech.
consensus.zh: 领导者必须贴近客户和产品，尤其是在消费科技领域。

contrarian.en: Humanity and societal adoption matter more than technology itself in AI deployment.
contrarian.zh: 在AI部署中，人性和社会接受度比技术本身更重要。

contrarian.en: Network effects alone are insufficient as a moat; ecosystems and hardware are needed.
contrarian.zh: 仅靠网络效应作为护城河是不够的；还需要生态系统和硬件。

contrarian.en: Designers should be the bottleneck for shipping products to ensure cohesive experience.
contrarian.zh: 设计师应成为产品发布的瓶颈，以确保体验的一致性。

contrarian.en: AR glasses should not be notification-based; they should anchor content in the world.
contrarian.zh: AR眼镜不应基于通知；而应将内容锚定在现实世界中。

contrarian.en: Snap's middle-child position in market is a strength, not a weakness.
contrarian.zh: Snap在市场中的中间孩子位置是优势而非劣势。

transcript.sec: Why building a durable social consumer product is hard

turns.en: You guys have a billion monthly active users. Why is it so freaking hard to build a durable lasting social consumer product? So much of consumer technology focuses on drive product market fit. People don't spend nearly enough time thinking about distribution and figuring out distribution.
turns.zh: 你们有 10 亿月活用户。为什么打造一个持久耐用的社交消费产品这么难？太多消费科技公司只关注产品市场匹配，人们花在思考分发和搞定分发上的时间远远不够。

turns.en: Well, I think it's really interesting, you know, in terms of the examples that you just shared, right? Of TikTok and Threads, because you just shared two examples of people who figured out distribution. And I think that's actually one of the hardest things to figure out in consumer technology today. We were so fortunate when we created Snapchat, the mobile phone and the App Store were just getting started. So people were downloading lots of new apps all the time. They were really excited about trying new services. Instagram, I think it started a year before Snapchat or something like that. So there was a real appetite to try new apps and new services. And that's not the case today. It's a lot harder to get distribution for new ideas and new services. People aren't downloading as many apps now as they used to. And both TikTok and Threads figured out distribution, which is why I think they're more recent examples of success. TikTok did it with money, which I actually thought was really innovative. They spent billions of dollars subsidizing both sides of their video marketplace, right? Acquiring customers to watch videos and then paying creators to make videos. And so they were able to bootstrap their ecosystem. And I think with Threads, obviously, they were able to leverage the amazing distribution that Meta has across all of their other products. So I think so much of consumer technology focuses on, you know, am I building the right product? Do I have product market fit? Have I built something that's really going to resonate with customers that they're really going to want to use all the time? And I think people don't spend nearly enough time thinking about distribution and figuring out distribution. And that seems to me to be a huge differentiator. So, you know, as I reflect on the early days of building Snapchat, the thing that we figured out in terms of distribution, especially when it comes to social, was that, you know, back then people believed that there were network effects in social networks that meant that the more people you had using the service, the stickier it is, right? And so there was no way to beat the biggest networks because those were the most valuable. And what Snapchat figured out that was, I think, different was that, despite the fact that there were much bigger networks that connected more people, what really mattered was connecting you to the right people. And so if you could just connect someone not to all their friends, but to their best friend, to their partner, to their spouse, the people that they cared most about in the world, that's where the majority of the value is in the network. And so that's what really allowed us to grow in those early days. We had the benefit of the App Store and mobile distribution, but we also had a different way of growing our service, which wasn't about having the most friends, it was about having your close friends.
turns.zh: 嗯，我觉得这很有意思，就拿你刚才举的例子来说吧，TikTok 和 Threads，你举了两个搞定了分发的例子。我认为这其实是当今消费科技领域最难解决的问题之一。我们创建 Snapchat 时非常幸运，手机和 App Store 才刚刚起步。所以人们一直在大量下载新应用，他们对尝试新服务非常兴奋。Instagram 大概比 Snapchat 早一年上线。所以当时人们对尝试新应用和新服务有真正的热情。但今天不是这样了。为新想法和新服务获取分发要难得多。人们现在下载的应用没有以前多了。而 TikTok 和 Threads 都搞定了分发，这就是为什么我认为它们是更近期的成功案例。TikTok 是用钱做到的，我其实觉得这很有创新性。他们花了数十亿美元补贴视频市场的两端，对吧？获取用户来看视频，然后付费给创作者制作视频。这样他们就能启动自己的生态系统。至于 Threads，显然他们利用了 Meta 在所有其他产品上的惊人分发能力。所以我认为太多消费科技公司只关注：我是不是在打造正确的产品？我有没有产品市场匹配？我有没有做出真正能引起用户共鸣、让他们一直想用的东西？而我认为人们花在思考和搞定分发上的时间远远不够。这在我看来是一个巨大的差异化因素。所以，回顾 Snapchat 早期的日子，我们在分发方面搞明白的一件事，尤其是在社交方面，就是当时人们相信社交网络存在网络效应，意味着使用服务的人越多，粘性就越强，对吧？所以无法击败最大的网络，因为它们最有价值。而 Snapchat 搞明白的不同之处在于，尽管存在连接更多人的更大网络，真正重要的是把你和正确的人连接起来。所以如果你能把一个人连接起来，不是连接所有朋友，而是连接他们最好的朋友、伴侣、配偶，那些他们最在乎的人，那就是网络中大部分价值的所在。这就是我们在早期能够增长的原因。我们受益于 App Store 和移动分发，但我们也有一种不同的服务增长方式，不是关于拥有最多朋友，而是关于拥有你的亲密朋友。

transcript.secZh: 打造耐用社交产品为何难

turns.en: Like Snap has always been punching above its weight in terms of just how much new stuff comes out of your team. Stories, AR glasses, swipe base navigation.
turns.zh: Snap 在创新产出上一直超常发挥，比如 Stories、AR 眼镜、滑动导航。

turns.en: We have a very very small design team that is constantly innovating and creating new things.
turns.zh: 我们有一个非常非常小的设计团队，他们一直在创新和创造新东西。

turns.en: Your first day that you join the design team, you present work. You're making things. If you want to have a good idea, you have to have lots of ideas.
turns.zh: 你加入设计团队的第一天就要展示作品。你一直在做东西。如果你想有一个好主意，你必须有很多主意。

transcript.sec: Being copied and software as a moat

turns.en: People copy you. As a human, how does it feel just to see this consistently happen?
turns.zh: 人们抄袭你。作为一个人，看到这种事一再发生，你是什么感受？

turns.en: 15 years ago, we essentially learned that software is not a moat, which is something that everyone is discovering today with AI.
turns.zh: 15 年前，我们基本上就学到了软件不是护城河，这一点今天每个人都在 AI 领域重新发现。

transcript.sec: The crucible moment and humanity vs technology

turns.en: You describe this coming year as the crucible moment. It's a real turning point.
turns.zh: 你把即将到来的一年描述为关键时刻。这是一个真正的转折点。

turns.en: We're in an industry where so much of the conversation is focused on technology, humanity is far more important. Because humanity dictates how technology is adopted. Technology leaders think that folks will just blindly adopt new technology as it comes out. There's going to be a huge amount of societal pushback on a lot of the changes that are coming with AI.
turns.zh: 我们身处一个行业，其中大量讨论都聚焦于技术，但人性要重要得多。因为人性决定了技术如何被采用。技术领导者认为人们会盲目地接受任何新出现的技术。但即将到来的 AI 变革会遭遇巨大的社会阻力。

turns.en: Today my guest is Evan Spiegel, CEO and co-founder of Snap. Evan is one of the very few humans in the world who has successfully built and scaled a lasting consumer social app. In the 15 years since Snapchat launched, there are essentially zero social consumer apps that have launched and stuck around. Snapchat has over 1 billion monthly active users, is generating over $6 billion a year in revenue. People post over 8 billion AR lens photos a day on Snapchat. And over the years, Evan and his team have invented the concept of stories. They had the first AR glasses product in the market. They invented swipe base navigation, the camera being primary. And also back in the day, face swapping, making people look older, and so many of the things that are just copied throughout the entire industry. If this is truly the golden age of consumer products, like many people say AI is going to enable, there's a lot that we can learn about how Evan and his team think and operate and are able to continue innovating. This is a rare podcast interview with Evan and we cover a lot of ground. Before we get into it, don't forget to check out Lenny's Product Pass .com for a year free of the hottest and most well-crafted AI products in the world available exclusively to Lenny's Newsletter subscribers. With that, I bring you Evan Spiegel. Evan, thank you so much for being here and welcome to the podcast.
turns.zh: 今天的嘉宾是 Evan Spiegel，Snap 的 CEO 兼联合创始人。Evan 是世界上极少数成功打造并规模化持久消费社交应用的人之一。在 Snapchat 上线后的 15 年里，几乎没有新的社交消费应用出现并存活下来。Snapchat 拥有超过 10 亿月活用户，年营收超过 60 亿美元。用户每天在 Snapchat 上发布超过 80 亿张 AR 镜头照片。多年来，Evan 和他的团队发明了 Stories 的概念，推出了市场上第一款 AR 眼镜产品，发明了滑动导航、以相机为核心，还有当年的换脸、变老特效等等，这些都被整个行业抄袭。如果这真的是消费产品的黄金时代——就像很多人说 AI 将带来的那样——那么我们可以从 Evan 和他的团队如何思考、运作并持续创新中学到很多。这是一次罕见的播客访谈，我们聊了很多内容。在开始之前，别忘了访问 Lenny's Product Pass .com，可以免费一年使用全球最热门、最精良的 AI 产品，仅限 Lenny's Newsletter 订阅者。那么，有请 Evan Spiegel。Evan，非常感谢你来做客，欢迎来到播客。

turns.en: Thank you so much. Thanks for having me. I'm looking forward to it.
turns.zh: 非常感谢。谢谢邀请。我很期待。

transcript.sec: AI's role in product development and distribution

turns.en: If you think about just where AI has come into the market, it started originally just like helping people autocomplete code. It's something I've been talking a lot on this podcast. So, it's like, okay, autocomplete code. Okay, now it's writing all our code. Okay, now it's reviewing our code, and now it's testing itself. And now it's going to help us come up with ideas. So, it's like starting in the middle of the product development process and expanding out. And I feel like it will be very good at coming up with great ideas and developing strategy. Like it's going up the funnel or the timeline of building a product. And I feel like distribution is where it ends up being what ends up being the new moat and the new biggest challenge because AI is not going to really help you there. So, that's really interesting that it's even more so true for consumer products.
turns.zh: 如果你想想 AI 进入市场的方式，它最初只是帮助人们自动补全代码。我在这个播客里经常聊这个。所以，先是自动补全代码，然后现在它写我们所有的代码，再然后它审查我们的代码，现在它自己测试代码。接下来它还会帮我们想点子。所以它从产品开发流程的中间开始，然后向外扩展。我觉得它会非常擅长想出好主意和制定策略，就像沿着产品构建的漏斗或时间线向上走。而我觉得分销最终会成为新的护城河和最大的挑战，因为 AI 在这方面帮不了你太多。所以这很有意思，尤其是在消费产品领域更是如此。

turns.en: I think that's absolutely the case. And the only thing I would say is, the most exciting times in technology are when there are new platforms that get created, and that tends to be where a lot of the value comes from. You think about a lot of the big consumer technology companies today, they were really born out of mobile, right? Whether it's Uber, Snapchat, some of those early generation companies that really got distribution and built themselves on mobile. And so, I think as we look forward to these next generation form factors, things like glasses, there's going to be a whole new set of opportunities and a whole new surface for people to build generational consumer companies. And I'm really excited about that.
turns.zh: 我完全同意。我只想说一点，科技领域最激动人心的时刻是当新平台被创造出来的时候，而价值往往就来自那里。想想今天很多大型消费科技公司，它们其实都诞生于移动时代，对吧？无论是 Uber、Snapchat，还是那些早期真正抓住分销机会并在移动端建立起来的公司。所以，当我们展望下一代形态，比如眼镜这样的设备时，将会有一整套全新的机会和全新的界面，让人们去打造一代人的消费公司。我对此非常兴奋。

turns.en: Especially if they have distribution already. Feels like that's going to be like I'm just worried for startups. It's going to be very hard to get distribution just like it's there's so much launching every day. So much to pay attention to, and there just feels very hard for a startup to get any attention with existing incumbents.
turns.zh: 尤其是如果它们已经有分销渠道的话。感觉这就像……我只是为初创公司担心。获得分销会非常困难，因为每天都有太多东西发布，太多东西需要关注，初创公司很难在现有巨头面前获得任何关注。
